html:
Roofing Verhalen's Farm Buildings and Highway 17 Traffic

Verhalen sits in the irrigated farmland between Pecos and the country toward Balmorhea, land that's grown the area's well-known cantaloupes, alfalfa, and cotton for generations under the district's irrigation system. Highway 17 carries a steady flow of Delaware Basin oilfield traffic straight through it. Our roofing work here splits between that farm building stock and the buildings serving traffic passing through on the way to the oilfield.

Irrigation-District Farm Buildings

The building stock in Verhalen reflects a working farm economy first. Packing sheds handle melons and produce during the season, cotton module storage sits near gin-adjacent buildings, and equipment barns house the irrigation-era machinery that keeps the district's fields watered. None of it was built with much margin for roof failure going unnoticed, since a leak into a packing shed or a module storage building can ruin what's stored underneath.

These buildings also carry a wide range of ages, since irrigation farming here goes back decades and buildings get patched and expanded rather than replaced on any predictable schedule.

Pump houses tied to the district's canal and well infrastructure round out the building count, usually small structures but ones where a roof leak reaching electrical equipment for a well pump can shut down water delivery to a field at exactly the wrong point in the season. We treat those buildings with the same urgency an oilfield operator would treat a compressor building, even though the equipment inside is worth a fraction as much.

What Farm Roofs Deal With Here

Large clear-span equipment barns and pole-barn structures are the most common building type, and their low-slope gutter lines are where we find the most ponding, particularly on buildings that have had a lean-to addition tacked onto one side over the years. Packing shed roofs need enough ventilation to manage produce storage without letting dust and grit blow straight in, which is a tighter balance than it sounds.

A lot of these buildings are seasonal in how they're used even if they stand year-round. A packing shed that sits closed most of the year and gets reopened hard for harvest season is exactly the kind of building where a small leak goes unnoticed until the building is suddenly back in daily use and it's too late to plan the repair calmly.

Roof Calls We Handle in Verhalen

The pattern of calls we get out of Verhalen's farm buildings looks like this:

  • Wind-lifted metal panels on equipment barns during spring dust and wind events
  • Leaks discovered right before harvest season when packing sheds reopen
  • Hail damage on cotton module storage roofs
  • Rust at roof penetrations near irrigation pump houses from constant moisture exposure
  • Ridge cap failures on older farm structures never re-fastened since original construction
  • Ponding water at low-slope additions built onto original barns over the years

Timing an inspection ahead of harvest catches most of these before they turn into a bigger problem, which is why we push a roof inspection scheduled around the growing season rather than waiting for a call after damage is already done.

Highway 17's Steady Oilfield Traffic and What It Brings

Highway 17 running through Verhalen has picked up a second job on top of its old one as a farm-to-market road. It carries a steady stream of lease traffic connecting to Pecos-area oilfield work alongside the seasonal surge of harvest trucks moving produce and cotton. Buildings sitting directly on the highway frontage take on a mixed exposure that a building set back on the farm doesn't see: more constant vibration, more dust, and more wear on roof edges facing the road.

We treat frontage buildings differently from buildings set back on the property for exactly that reason, checking edge and fastener condition more often where highway exposure is constant rather than seasonal.

Timing Farm Roof Work Around the Growing Season

Unlike an oilfield building that needs attention the moment a problem shows up, a farm building's roof repair often has to wait for a window between planting and harvest when the building isn't in daily use. We plan around that rather than pushing an owner to interrupt harvest season for a repair that can hold until the building is idle again.

That timing flexibility matters more to farm operators here than fast emergency response, and we schedule Verhalen work with that difference in mind.

Questions We Get About Roofing Work in Verhalen

Do you work on packing sheds and produce storage buildings?

Yes, and we pay close attention to ventilation details so a repair doesn't create a moisture or dust problem inside a building storing produce.

Why do farm building leaks often show up right at harvest time?

Because the building sits closed most of the year. A leak that started months earlier only becomes obvious once the building is back in daily use during harvest.

Is farm roofing different from oilfield building roofing?

Yes. The materials and failure points overlap, but the usage pattern and scheduling needs are different, and we plan farm work around the growing season rather than treating it as an emergency call by default.

Does a roof need to come off during harvest season, or can work wait?

In most cases it can wait. We time non-emergency farm roof work for the window between planting and harvest whenever possible.

Do buildings right on Highway 17 need different attention than ones further back on the farm?

Yes. Highway frontage buildings see more constant vibration and dust exposure, so we check edge and fastener condition on those more frequently.
